failure_scenarios.description: A rapid geopolitical de-escalation in the Strait of Hormuz — via US-Iran ceasefire or diplomatic back-channel deal brokered by Gulf states — collapses gold's fear premium simultaneously with an energy price crash, removing both the safe-haven and inflation-hedge pillars of the GLD thesis.
failure_scenarios.reasoning: Gold is currently pricing in a compounding geopolitical risk premium from the Hormuz crisis — if Iran's selective ship-transit pricing ($2M/crossing) signals a willingness to negotiate, and Saudi Arabia's successful Yanbu rerouting reduces urgency for military escalation, a ceasefire scenario becomes plausible within weeks. A de-escalation would simultaneously crash oil (removing inflation expectations), strengthen risk appetite (killing the safe-haven bid), and potentially prompt a hawkish Fed

failure_scenarios.description: The portfolio's internal correlation structure collapses as DBC (20.3%) and GLD (16.9%) surge on energy/inflation fear while HYG simultaneously craters, exposing the false diversification assumption and amplifying drawdown beyond simple position sizing.
failure_scenarios.reasoning: The current portfolio holds HYG (credit risk/duration) alongside DBC (commodity inflation hedge) and GLD (crisis hedge) — a construction that implicitly assumes these assets provide offsetting risk. In a 1970s-style stagflationary shock triggered by the Hormuz closure, DBC and GLD would rally hard while HYG sells off sharply, meaning the portfolio hedges are working AGAINST the HYG thesis rather than cushioning it. failure_scenarios.description: HYG suffers a liquidity-driven forced-seller cascade as ETF outflows accelerate in a risk-off episode, with the ETF's price dislocating below NAV and triggering programmatic selling that overshoots fundamental credit deterioration.
failure_scenarios.reasoning: HYG as an ETF wrapper on illiquid underlying HY bonds creates a structural fragility: retail and institutional flows in/out of the ETF can exceed the liquidity of the underlying bonds in stress scenarios, forcing authorized participants to sell bonds into a thin market.
